summary: Tractate Yoma daf 80b covers: Cheekful debate between Rav and Shmuel; method of measuring; All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK; Reish Lakish: overeating on YK creates liability even without swallowing.

display.whats: Cheekful debate between Rav and Shmuel; method of measuring
display.hint: Rav and Shmuel debate the cheekful measure: how to determine its size, and the story of how they determined each other's measure.

learning.summary: Rav and Shmuel debate the cheekful measure: how to determine its size, and the story of how they determined each other's measure.
learning.ahaMoment: Rav and Shmuel personally measured each other's cheeks - the law required an empirical test that could only be done between friends willing to put things in each other's mouths.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rav and Shmuel measured each other's cheeks: the personalized measure required personal testing.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about cheekful debate between rav and shmuel; method of measuring?

argumentFlow.label: The cheekful question concerns even Og of Bashan
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The earlier puzzle, that a cheekful seems smaller than Beit Shammai's quarter-log, is resolved: the question was posed even for Og, king of Bashan, whose enormous cheekful far exceeds a quarter-log. For him Beit Shammai's quarter-log is the lenient measure, so it is a genuine Beit Shammai stringency only for ordinary people.

argumentFlow.label: Why is drinking relative but eating fixed?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Zeira objects: why is the eating measure a fixed large date for everyone, while the drinking measure varies with each person's mouth? Abaye answers that the tradition fixes the date as the amount that settles the mind, whereas for drinking only one's own cheekful settles one's mind, not a smaller person's.

argumentFlow.label: Should eating also be relative to body size?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Zeira presses: is an average person and Og both satisfied by a large date alike? Abaye answers that the tradition simply fixes the large date as settling the mind; an average person's mind is greatly settled and Og's only slightly, but the date relieves affliction for anyone.

argumentFlow.label: Should the food's richness change the measure?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Zeira objects further: fatty meat settles the mind at a large date, but would the same volume of grapevine shoots settle it equally? Abaye answers that the tradition fixes the date-measure regardless: rich meat settles the mind greatly, lean shoots only slightly, but the measure stands.

argumentFlow.label: Why no longer eating-time for the larger Yom Kippur measure?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava objects that ordinary prohibitions require an olive-bulk eaten within the time to eat a half-loaf, yet Yom Kippur requires the larger large-date amount in that same span, which seems lenient. Abaye answers that the tradition ties liability to eating within that duration, since only then does the mind settle.

argumentFlow.label: The impurity measure is larger still in the same time
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava objects that impure food renders one impure at two egg-bulks within the half-loaf time, a far larger amount in the same span. Rav Pappa answers that impurity of the body from eating is only rabbinic, so the Sages were lenient and the comparison does not bind.

argumentFlow.label: Is impurity of the body Torah or rabbinic law?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara challenges Rav Pappa from his own statement deriving bodily impurity from a verse, which suggests Torah law. It resolves that Rav Pappa held it is actually rabbinic, and the verse he cited serves only as a support, not a true Torah source.

theological: Rav and Shmuel personally measured each other's cheeks - the law required an emp
narrative: Cheekful debate between Rav and Shmuel; method of measuring

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners expect the eating and drinking measures to follow the same logic, both fixed or both relative.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara holds them apart: eating is a fixed large date for everyone because that amount settles the mind, while drinking is relative to each person's cheekful, because only one's own mouthful settles one's own mind. The asymmetry is deliberate, not an inconsistency.

visualizableElements.name: Cheekful debate between Rav and Shmuel; method of

quizSeeds.q: How is the apparent problem that a cheekful is smaller than Beit Shammai's quarter-log resolved?
quizSeeds.a: The cheekful measure was set even for someone as large as Og, king of Bashan, whose mouthful far exceeds a quarter-log. For such a person Beit Shammai's fixed quarter-log is the more lenient measure, so the case stands as a real, if narrow, Beit Shammai stringency.

quizSeeds.q: Why does Abaye hold the eating measure is a fixed large date while drinking varies by person?
quizSeeds.a: Because the accepted tradition fixes the large date as the amount that settles anyone's mind and relieves affliction, even if it settles a large person only slightly. For drinking, by contrast, only one's own cheekful settles one's mind, so the measure must scale to each individual's mouth.

quizSeeds.q: How does Rav Pappa defend the larger impurity measure eaten within the same time span?
quizSeeds.a: He argues that impurity of the body from eating impure food is only rabbinic, so the Sages were lenient with its measure. Therefore it cannot be compared to the Torah-level Yom Kippur measure, and the verse he had cited for bodily impurity serves only as a support.

sugyot.finalRuling: Rav and Shmuel debate the cheekful measure: how to determine its size, and the story of how they determined each other's measure.

display.whats: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK
display.hint: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK - you cannot eat small amounts of multiple foods and avoid liability.

learning.summary: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK - you cannot eat small amounts of multiple foods and avoid liability.
learning.ahaMoment: Combination of foods: YK's eating prohibition is about the act of eating, not specific foods - all edibles count together.
learning.memoryAnchor: All foods combine: YK eating is about the act, not the type of food.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about all foods combine toward the eating measure on yk?

argumentFlow.label: Foods combine, including salt and brine
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The mishna says all foods combine toward the liability measure. Rav Pappa rules meat and the salt on it combine to a large date, since people eat them together. Reish Lakish adds that brine on a vegetable combines with it, teaching that even something that merely prepares food for eating counts as food, not a separate beverage.

theological: Combination of foods: YK's eating prohibition is about the act of eating, not sp
narrative: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of food combination context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume liquid condiments like brine are drinks and so cannot count toward the food-eating measure.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara rules that brine, which prepares the vegetable for eating, is treated as food rather than a beverage, so it combines with the vegetable toward the large-date liability for eating on Yom Kippur.

visualizableElements.name: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK

quizSeeds.q: Why does brine on a vegetable combine with the vegetable toward the Yom Kippur measure?
quizSeeds.a: Although one might think brine is a beverage that does not combine with food, the Gemara teaches that anything which prepares food for eating is itself considered food. Since the brine readies the vegetable for eating, it joins it toward the large-date measure.

sugyot.finalRuling: All foods combine toward the eating measure on YK - you cannot eat small amounts of multiple foods and avoid liability.

display.whats: Reish Lakish: overeating on YK creates liability even without swallowing
display.hint: Reish Lakish rules that chewing food on YK without swallowing still creates liability if the mouth is filled to the cheekful measure.

learning.summary: Reish Lakish rules that chewing food on YK without swallowing still creates liability if the mouth is filled to the cheekful measure.
learning.ahaMoment: Chewing without swallowing: intent to eat and the physical act of filling the mouth triggers liability - the prohibition is not about nutrition but about the act of pleasurable eating.
learning.memoryAnchor: Reish Lakish: chewing counts, swallowing is not required for liability.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about reish lakish: overeating on yk creates liability even withou?

argumentFlow.label: Excessive eating on Yom Kippur is exempt
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: Reish Lakish rules that one who eats to excess on Yom Kippur, forcing food down past satiety, is exempt, because the verse speaks of a soul 'afflicted' and one cut off for not being afflicted, which excludes one who harms rather than benefits himself by such eating.

argumentFlow.label: The same principle applied to teruma
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yirmeya in the name of Reish Lakish applies the principle to teruma: a non-priest who eats teruma to excess pays the principal but not the added fifth, since the verse 'if a man eat' excludes one who harms himself rather than eats normally. He still pays the principal because the priest suffered a loss.

theological: Chewing without swallowing: intent to eat and the physical act of filling the mo
narrative: Reish Lakish: overeating on YK creates liability even withou

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of Reish Lakish context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume any eating on Yom Kippur, however done, incurs the full penalty.
misconceptions.correction: Reish Lakish exempts eating done to excess that harms the eater, because the Torah's language of 'affliction' targets normal, beneficial eating. The same logic exempts the added fifth for one who overeats teruma, since that too is self-harm rather than ordinary eating.

visualizableElements.name: Reish Lakish: overeating on YK creates liability e

quizSeeds.q: Why is one who eats to excess on Yom Kippur exempt from liability?
quizSeeds.a: Because the prohibition is framed as failing to be 'afflicted,' and eating past satiety to the point of harming oneself is not the normal, enjoyable eating the verse addresses. One who forces food down beyond fullness is harming himself, not benefiting, so he is excluded.

quizSeeds.q: How does Reish Lakish's principle carry over to a non-priest who eats teruma excessively?
quizSeeds.a: Such a person pays the principal but not the added fifth. The verse 'if a man eat' refers to normal eating and excludes one who harms himself by overeating, so the penalty fifth does not apply, though he still repays the principal because the priest suffered a real loss.

sugyot.finalRuling: Reish Lakish rules that chewing food on YK without swallowing still creates liability if the mouth is filled to the cheekful measure.

rashiTranslations.he: כי איתשיל. בבי מדרשא הך דלעיל כמה ישתה ויהא חייב לענין עוג
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Rav's opinion on the size of the cheekful measure.

rashiTranslations.he: מלך הבשן כלומר לענין אדם כענקים איתשיל והוו להו בית שמאי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Shmuel's approach to measuring the cheekful.

rashiTranslations.he: לחומרא דמחייבי ברביעית: כזית בכדי אכילת פרס. קים לן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the story of Rav and Shmuel measuring each other's cheeks.

rashiTranslations.he: (כריתות דף יב:) בכל אותן איסורין ששיעורן בכזית שאם שהה בין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: all foods combine toward the YK eating measure.

rashiTranslations.he: תחילת אכילת השיעור לגמר אכילתו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Reish Lakish - chewing without swallowing triggers liability.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.he: סעודות כדתנן חציה לבית המנוגע
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.he: שתלה הכתוב שהייתו לטמא בגדים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.he: פרס. בתמיה יום הכפורים ששיעור
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 80b.

rashiTranslations.he: אכילתו בככותבת אף הוא שיעורו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: פרס ותו לא כיון דשיעורא רבה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הוא בטפי מהכי ליצטרף: הנח
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לטומאת גויה דלאו דאורייתא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ואקילו רבנן בה: ולא תטמאו בהם.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: גבי אכילה כתיב אל תשקצו את
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: נפשותיכם דהיינו אכילה ולא תטמאו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בהם לא זו היא אזהרת טומאה שהרי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אין ישראל מוזהרין מליטמא דכתיב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אמור אל הכהנים וגו' (ויקרא כא)
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כהנים מוזהרין ואין ישראל מוזהרין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אלא אזהרת אכילתן וכתיב ונטמתם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בם משמע שעל ידי אכילתן תטמאו:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: מכאן לטומאת גויה. על ידי אוכלין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: טמאין דאורייתא: מדרבנן. דקרא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: impure by Torah law. 'Rabbinically' - for the verse

rashiTranslations.he: לאו לטומאה אתא שאין לך דבר
rashiTranslations.en: does not come to teach impurity, since the only thing that

rashiTranslations.he: מטמא אדם על ידי אכילה אלא נבלת
rashiTranslations.en: conveys impurity to a person through eating is the carcass

rashiTranslations.he: עוף טהור: אלא אסמכתא בעלמא.
rashiTranslations.en: of a clean bird. 'Rather, it is mere support (asmachta)' -

rashiTranslations.he: ועיקר קרא דרשינן בפרק אמר להם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: and the main verse we expound in the chapter 'The appointed officer said to them'

rashiTranslations.he: הממונה (לעיל דף לט.) אדם מטמא
rashiTranslations.en: (above 39a): a person conveys a little impurity to himself,

rashiTranslations.he: עצמו מעט מטמאין אותו הרבה:
rashiTranslations.en: and they convey much impurity to him.

rashiTranslations.he: אומצא ומילחא. שעליה: אכשורי אוכלא. משקה הבא למתק את
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'umtza u-milcha' - the salt that is on it. 'Ikhsharei ukhla' - a liquid that comes to sweeten

rashiTranslations.he: האוכל אוכלא הוא: אכילה גסה. שאכל לילי יום הכפורים על
rashiTranslations.en: the food is itself considered food. 'Gross eating' - one who ate on the eve of Yom Kippur

rashiTranslations.he: השבע שהיה שבע מסעודה שהפסיק בה וכל מה שאכל משחשיכה
rashiTranslations.en: while already sated, having been full from a meal he had paused, so that all he ate after dark

rashiTranslations.he: אכילה גסה היתה ושלא להנאה: פרט למזיק. לזה שאינו מבטל ממנו
rashiTranslations.en: was gross eating and gave no pleasure. 'Excluding one who harms' - this one, for whom this eating relieves

rashiTranslations.he: שום עינוי על ידי אכילה זו אלא מזיק הוא את האוכלין ואת עצמו:
rashiTranslations.en: no affliction but only harms him - it damages the food and himself.

rashiTranslations.he: זר שאכל תרומה. בשוגג שחייבו הכתוב תשלומי קרן וחומש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'a non-kohen who ate teruma' - inadvertently, whom Scripture obligated to pay principal plus a fifth -

rashiTranslations.he: פטור אם אכלה על השבע: פרט למזיק. את התרומה:
rashiTranslations.en: is exempt if he ate it while sated. 'Excluding one who harms' - the teruma.

rashiTranslations.he: שכוסס
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi begins the next comment ('she-koses'), opening the following passage.
